steps.title: Identify the opening em-dash
steps.content: An opening em-dash appears before "first published": …—first published in [BLANK] is essential... The opening punctuation signals a parenthetical aside requiring a matching close.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice
steps.content:
Rule: an opening em-dash must be closed by a matching em-dash to form a complete parenthetical aside.

A — "1689—" Closes the dash: “—first published in 1689—is essential reading.” The parenthetical is complete and the main clause reads smoothly. Correct.

B — "1689" No closing dash. The aside opened by the first em-dash is never closed, leaving the sentence structurally broken. Fails.

C — "1689," A comma cannot close an em-dash parenthetical. The opening dash has no match. Fails.

D — "1689:" A colon cannot close an em-dash parenthetical. The structure remains open. Fails.

A is correct.
